raw_response:
Looking at this causal inference problem, I need to identify confounders - variables that affect both insurance type (treatment assignment) and in-hospital mortality (outcome), while avoiding mediators and colliders.

For insurance type assignment, key factors include:
- Demographics (age, race, marital status, language) - these relate to eligibility and socioeconomic status
- Baseline health status/comorbidities - sicker patients may be more likely to qualify for Medicaid
- Time period - policy changes over time

For in-hospital mortality, relevant factors include:
- Demographics (age is strongly predictive)
- All serious comorbidities that increase mortality risk

I'll exclude variables that are likely mediators (pathways through which insurance affects mortality, like quality of care received) or colliders (variables caused by both treatment and outcome).

ADJUSTMENT SET:

age_at_admission, sex, race_white, race_black, race_hispanic, race_asian, race_other, language_english, marital_status_married, period_2011_2013, period_2014_2016, period_2017_2019, period_2020_2022, chf, arrhythmia, valvular, pulm_circ, pvd, hypertension, paralysis, neuro_other, copd, diabetes_uncomplicated, diabetes_complicated, hypo_thyroid, renal_failure, liver_disease, peptic_ulcer, hiv, lymphoma, metastatic_cancer, solid_tumor, rheumatoid, coagulopathy, obesity, weight_loss, fluid_electrolyte, blood_loss_anemia, deficiency_anemia, alcohol_abuse, drug_abuse, psychosis, depression
